section.title: Ananias and Sapphira and the Apostles Persecuted
section.sectionRationale: Set against the shining generosity that closed chapter 4, this chapter opens with a lie at the heart of the church: Ananias and Sapphira pretend to give everything while secretly keeping back part of the price, and God strikes them down, so that great fear falls on the church. Then the apostles heal many, are arrested by the jealous high priest, freed by an angel, and hauled again before the Sanhedrin, where they declare they must obey God rather than men and testify to the risen and exalted Christ. Gamaliel's cautious counsel spares their lives; they are flogged, and they leave rejoicing to have suffered for the Name.
section.thesis: The holy God who fills His church with His Spirit will not be lied to or trifled with, and the same risen Christ whom He has exalted as Prince and Savior gives His witnesses a courage that outlasts prison, angelic rescue, threats, and flogging, so that the persecuted apostles rejoice to suffer for the Name and never stop proclaiming that Jesus is the Christ.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the deadly deceit of Ananias and Sapphira; the healing power and growth of the church; the arrest of the apostles and their release by an angel; their bold testimony before the council that they must obey God rather than men; and Gamaliel's counsel, the flogging, and the apostles' joy in suffering for the Name.

units.label: The deadly deceit of Ananias and Sapphira
units.note: a lie to the Holy Spirit, and great fear falls on the church

verses.text: Now a man named Ananias, together with his wife Sapphira, also sold a piece of property.
With his wife’s full knowledge, he {{nosphizomai|kept back}} some of the proceeds for himself, but brought a portion and laid it at the apostles’ feet.
Then Peter said, “Ananias, how is it that Satan has filled your heart to {{pseudomai|lie}} to the Holy Spirit and withhold some of the proceeds from the land?
Did it not belong to you before it was sold? And after it was sold, was it not at your disposal? How could you conceive such a deed in your heart? You have not lied to men, but to God!”
On hearing these words, Ananias fell down and died. And great fear came over all who heard what had happened.
Then the young men stepped forward, wrapped up his body, and carried him out and buried him.
About three hours later his wife also came in, unaware of what had happened.
“Tell me,” said Peter, “is this the price you and your husband got for the land?” “Yes,” she answered, “that is the price.”
“How could you agree to test the Spirit of the Lord?” Peter replied. “Look, the feet of the men who buried your husband are at the door, and they will carry you out also.”
At that instant she fell down at his feet and died. Then the young men came in and, finding her dead, carried her out and buried her beside her husband.
And great fear came over the whole church and all who heard about these events.

units.label: Signs and wonders, and the church grows
units.note: the sick are healed and multitudes are added to the Lord

verses.text: The apostles performed many signs and wonders among the people, and with one accord the believers gathered together in Solomon’s Colonnade.
Although the people regarded them highly, no one else dared to join them.
Yet more and more believers were brought to the Lord, large numbers of both men and women.
As a result, people brought the sick into the streets and laid them on cots and mats, so that at least Peter’s shadow might fall on some of them as he passed by.
Crowds also gathered from the towns around Jerusalem, bringing the sick and those tormented by unclean spirits, and all of them were healed.

units.label: Arrested and freed by night
units.note: jealousy imprisons the apostles, but an angel opens the doors

verses.text: Then the high priest and all his associates, who belonged to the party of the Sadducees, were filled with jealousy. They went out
and arrested the apostles and put them in the public jail.
But during the night an angel of the Lord opened the doors of the jail and brought them out, saying,
“Go, stand in the temple courts and tell the people the full message of this new life.”

At daybreak the apostles entered the temple courts as they had been told and began to teach the people. When the high priest and his associates arrived, they convened the Sanhedrin, the full assembly of the elders of Israel, and sent to the jail for the apostles.
But on arriving at the jail, the officers did not find them there. So they returned with the report:
“We found the jail securely locked, with the guards posted at the doors; but when we opened them, we found no one inside.”
When the captain of the temple guard and the chief priests heard this account, they were perplexed as to what was happening.
Then someone came in and announced, “Look, the men you put in jail are standing in the temple courts teaching the people!”
At that point, the captain went with the officers and brought the apostles, but not by force, for fear the people would stone them.

units.label: We must obey God rather than men
units.note: the exalted Prince and Savior grants repentance to Israel

verses.text: They brought them in and made them stand before the Sanhedrin, where the high priest interrogated them.
“We gave you strict orders not to teach in this name,” he said. “Yet you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching and are determined to make us responsible for this man’s blood.”
But Peter and the other apostles replied, “We must {{peitharcheo|obey}} God rather than men.
The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom you had killed by hanging Him on a tree.
God exalted Him to His right hand as {{archegos|Prince}} and Savior, in order to grant repentance and forgiveness of sins to Israel.
We are witnesses of these things, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey Him.”

units.label: Gamaliel's counsel, the flogging, and joy in the Name
units.note: if it is from God you cannot stop it; the apostles rejoice to suffer

verses.text: When the Council members heard this, they were enraged, and they resolved to put the apostles to death.
But a Pharisee named Gamaliel, a teacher of the law who was honored by all the people, stood up in the Sanhedrin and ordered that the men be put outside for a short time.
“Men of Israel,” he said, “consider carefully what you are about to do to these men.
Some time ago Theudas rose up, claiming to be somebody, and about four hundred men joined him. He was killed, all his followers were dispersed, and it all came to nothing.
After him, Judas the Galilean appeared in the days of the census and drew away people after him. He too perished, and all his followers were scattered.
So in the present case I advise you: Leave these men alone. Let them go! For if their purpose or endeavor is of human origin, it will fail.
But if it is from God, you will not be able to stop them. You may even find yourselves fighting against God.”
At this, they yielded to Gamaliel. They called the apostles in and had them flogged. Then they ordered them not to speak in the name of Jesus, and released them.
The apostles left the Sanhedrin, rejoicing that they had been counted worthy of suffering disgrace for the Name.
Every day, in the temple courts and from house to house, they did not stop teaching and proclaiming the good news that Jesus is the Christ.

nosphizomai.gloss: To set apart secretly for oneself, to embezzle or pilfer, to keep back part of what should be handed over. The Greek Old Testament uses this very word for Achan, who kept back some of the devoted things at Jericho, Joshua 7. The echo is deliberate: like Achan, Ananias secretly withholds while pretending full surrender, and the sin brings death into the camp of God's holy people.

pseudomai.gloss: To lie, to speak falsehood, to deceive. Peter names the true nature of the couple's act: it was not a mere financial reservation but a lie, and a lie aimed not merely at the apostles but at the Holy Spirit and at God Himself. The sin was hypocrisy, staging a total gift to win praise while the heart held back, and pretending before the God who sees the heart.

peitharcheo.gloss: To obey a ruling authority, to submit to and follow orders. The apostles use it to state a settled principle: they must obey God rather than men. When the command of any human authority collides with the clear command of God, the Christian's higher allegiance is to God; obedience to Him is not rebellion but the deepest loyalty, and it may cost dearly.

archegos.gloss: Leader, originator, pioneer, prince; the one who goes first and opens the way for others to follow. God has exalted the crucified Jesus to His right hand as Archegos and Savior. He is the trailblazer of life, the founder of a new humanity, who leads His people through death into resurrection, and from His throne He grants repentance and forgiveness to Israel.

basic.context: Chapter 4 ended with the church's beautiful generosity and Barnabas laying his gift at the apostles' feet. Now Ananias and Sapphira imitate the outward act but with a lie underneath: they sell property, secretly keep part of the price, yet pretend to give it all. Peter exposes the deceit as a lie to the Holy Spirit, and both fall dead, so that great fear grips the church. Then the apostles heal many and are arrested by the jealous high priest, freed by an angel, and brought again before the council, where they refuse to stop preaching Jesus. Gamaliel urges caution, they are flogged, and they rejoice to suffer for the Name.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the Ananias story not as God punishing generosity but as God guarding the holiness and truthfulness of His new community at a foundational moment. The point is not the size of the gift but the lie. Then read the trial scenes as another round in the pattern begun in chapter 4: witness, arrest, deliverance, testimony, threat, and joy. The apostles' words in verses 29 to 32 give the heart of it: obey God rather than men, because God has raised and exalted Jesus.
basic.originalLanguages: The words sharpen the story. Ananias nosphizomai, kept back, secretly held part of the price, the same word used of Achan who kept back the devoted things at Jericho. Peter calls it a pseudomai, a lie, to the Holy Spirit. Before the council the apostles say they must peitharcheo, obey, God rather than men. And they proclaim that God exalted Jesus as archegos, Prince or Pioneer, and Savior, who grants repentance to Israel.
basic.typology: Ananias keeping back the devoted price recalls Achan, whose secret theft brought judgment into Israel, warning that hidden sin defiles God's people. The apostles freed from prison by an angel echo God's repeated rescues of His servants. Jesus exalted as Prince and Savior fulfills the promise of a ruler who leads His people to life, a new and greater Joshua and Moses combined.
basic.christ: The apostles' testimony centers on Jesus: the God of the fathers raised Him up after the leaders killed Him on a tree, and exalted Him to His right hand as Prince and Savior to grant repentance and forgiveness to Israel. He is the living Lord whom they cannot stop preaching, and the good news they proclaim daily is simply that Jesus is the Christ. Even the judgment on Ananias guards the honor of His Spirit.
basic.god: God is holy and cannot be deceived; He sees the heart and will not let a lie sit unchallenged at the foundation of His church. He is also the God who saves, sending an angel to open the prison, exalting Jesus, and giving His Spirit to those who obey Him. He governs even the councils of His enemies, so that if this work is from God, no one can stop it.
basic.crossReferences: Joshua 7 tells of Achan keeping back the devoted things, the pattern behind Ananias. Deuteronomy 21:23 lies behind hanging on a tree. Acts 4:19-20 states the same resolve to obey God rather than men. Philippians 2:9-11 celebrates the God who exalted Jesus to the highest place.
basic.oneStory: The exalted Christ pours out His Spirit on a holy people, and God will keep that people true, even by fearful judgment, while He carries the gospel forward against every effort to silence it. Prison cannot hold His witnesses, threats cannot quiet them, and floggings only turn to joy. From Jerusalem the word keeps advancing, driven by the risen Prince and Savior toward the ends of the earth.

context.literary: The chapter is built as a deliberate contrast. Barnabas, who truly gave all, stands against Ananias and Sapphira, who only pretended to. The judgment scene is followed by a summary of growth, then a second arrest and trial that mirrors and heightens the first in chapter 4. Luke loves such paired scenes; here the repetition shows the opposition hardening and the apostles' courage deepening, ending on the striking note of joy in suffering.
context.historical: Selling property to help the needy was an extraordinary act, and public praise likely followed such gifts, which explains the temptation to fake full generosity. The high priest and the Sadducees held real power and were provoked by jealousy at the apostles' influence. Gamaliel was among the most respected rabbis of the age, the teacher of Saul of Tarsus, and his measured counsel reflects a genuine strand of Pharisaic caution. Flogging, up to forty lashes less one, was a severe and shaming punishment.
context.canonical: The Achan echo binds Acts 5 to the story of Israel's conquest and the danger of hidden sin among God's people. The apostles' principle, obey God rather than men, becomes a charter text for Christian conscience under unjust authority. The exaltation of Jesus as Prince and Savior joins the New Testament's chorus about the enthroned Christ, seen also in Hebrews, where He is called the Pioneer of salvation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke means to show that God guards the truthfulness and holiness of His church, that the risen Christ reigns and gives His witnesses unstoppable courage, and that persecution cannot silence the gospel but only spreads it. He wants readers to feel both the fear of the Lord and the joy of those counted worthy to suffer for the Name.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes unique, foundational events. The abiding truths are clear: God is holy and hates hypocrisy, believers must obey God rather than men when they conflict, and suffering for Christ is an honor. But not every feature is a standing rule, which raises the question of how to read the deaths of Ananias and Sapphira.

debates.question: Is the sudden death of Ananias and Sapphira a pattern God will always follow, or a unique foundational judgment?

views.label: A unique, foundational sign, not the normal pattern
views.case: Like the judgment on Nadab and Abihu at the start of the priesthood or Achan at the entry to the land, this falls at the birth of the church to establish the holiness of the new community. Elsewhere the New Testament calls for church discipline and patient correction, not instant death, 1 Corinthians 5, Galatians 6:1; so the abiding lesson is the fearful seriousness of hypocrisy, not an expectation that liars will drop dead today.

views.label: A revealing window into how God always regards hypocrisy
views.case: The text says great fear came on the whole church, drawing a lasting lesson; God's holiness has not changed, and what is normally hidden is here made visible. Read this way, the passage warns every generation that lying to the Spirit is deadly serious, even when judgment is not immediate, lest the church presume on grace.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold that God is holy and takes hypocrisy with deadly seriousness; they differ on whether the immediate death is a repeatable pattern or a unique, founding sign whose enduring purpose is godly fear and honesty before God.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the sin is caught in one verb: Ananias nosphizomai, secretly kept back part of the price, the very word used of Achan's theft at Jericho, so that the hidden reservation is exposed as sacrilege. Peter names it a pseudomai, a lie, aimed at the Holy Spirit and at God. When the apostles stand before the council their answer turns on peitharcheo: they must obey God rather than men. And their gospel exalts Jesus as archegos, the Prince, Pioneer, and Author of life, and as Savior, enthroned at God's right hand to grant repentance and forgiveness.

typology.type: Ananias keeping back the devoted price
typology.fulfillment: Recalls Achan in Joshua 7, whose secret theft of devoted things brought death into Israel; both warn that hidden sin defiles God's holy people at a decisive hour.

typology.type: The apostles freed from prison by an angel
typology.fulfillment: Echoes God's rescues of His servants throughout Scripture and anticipates Peter's later deliverance in Acts 12; the God who reigns opens doors no jailer can keep shut.

typology.type: Jesus exalted as Prince and Savior
typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the hope of a ruler who leads His people to life; Hebrews 2:10 and 12:2 name Him the Pioneer or Author of salvation who has gone ahead through death into glory.

study.christ: The apostles preach one message: the God of the fathers raised up Jesus whom the leaders killed by hanging Him on a tree, and exalted Him to His right hand as Prince and Savior to grant repentance and forgiveness of sins to Israel. He is the risen, reigning Lord whose name they will not stop proclaiming. Even the awful scene with Ananias defends the honor of His Spirit, for to lie to the Spirit is to lie to God. The chapter closes with the church proclaiming daily the good news that Jesus is the Christ.
study.god: God is holy and true, and He will not permit deceit to fester at the foundation of His people; He sees what is secret and answers the lie with judgment, so that a healthy fear falls on the church. Yet the same God saves and vindicates: He sends His angel to open the prison, He exalts Jesus, and He gives His Spirit to those who obey Him. He rules over the plots of the high priest and the counsel of the Sanhedrin, so that His purpose, being from Him, cannot be stopped.

crossReferences.note: Achan secretly keeps back devoted things and brings judgment on Israel, the Old Testament pattern behind Ananias and Sapphira.
The apostles' resolve to obey God rather than men, stated first in the previous chapter and here made a settled principle.
Hanging on a tree; the apostles use this language of Jesus' crucifixion, which Paul develops in Galatians 3:13.
God highly exalted Jesus and gave Him the name above every name, matching His exaltation as Prince and Savior here.
Jesus the Pioneer and Perfecter of faith, the same title Archegos used of Him in Acts 5:31.

study.oneStory: The ascended Christ has sent His Spirit to form a holy people, and God will keep them holy, even through fearful judgment, while He drives the gospel outward against every attempt to stop it. The Prince and Savior reigns at God's right hand, granting repentance to Israel and giving His Spirit to those who obey. Prisons open, threats fail, and floggings turn to joy, as the unstoppable word moves from Jerusalem toward the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: What was the real sin of Ananias and Sapphira?

options.text: They lied to the Holy Spirit, pretending to give everything while secretly keeping back part of the price.
options.note: Peter names it a lie to God and the Spirit; the hypocrisy, not the amount, was the sin.

options.text: They kept back some of the money from the sale of their land.
options.note: They did keep back part, but Peter stresses the land and proceeds were theirs to keep; the sin was the lie, not the reservation.

options.text: They refused to sell their property for the church.
options.note: They did sell it; the problem was pretending to give all while holding back, not refusing to give.

options.text: They gave too little compared with the other believers.
options.note: The size of the gift was never the issue; Peter says it remained at their disposal.

quiz.q: Why did great fear come over the whole church?

options.text: Because God had shown, in the deaths of Ananias and Sapphira, that He will not be lied to or trifled with.
options.note: Verse 11 links the fear to God's holy judgment on the deceit.

options.text: Because two members of the church had suddenly died.
options.note: The deaths caused the fear, but the deeper cause was recognizing God's holiness and the seriousness of lying to Him.

options.text: Because the authorities were about to arrest everyone.
options.note: The fear arose from God's judgment within the church, not from the authorities at this point.

options.text: Because the apostles had lost their healing power.
options.note: The apostles went on to heal many; there was no loss of power.

quiz.q: How did the apostles answer the council's order to stop teaching in Jesus' name?

options.text: They said they must obey God rather than men, and testified that God raised and exalted Jesus.
options.note: Verses 29 to 32 give both the principle and the testimony.

options.text: They declared that they were witnesses of these things, along with the Holy Spirit.
options.note: True, from verse 32, but the governing answer is the principle that they must obey God rather than men.

options.text: They promised to teach only in private from house to house.
options.note: They kept teaching both in the temple and house to house; they made no such compromise.

options.text: They fell silent out of fear of the high priest.
options.note: They did the opposite, refusing to be silenced and rejoicing to suffer.

quiz.q: How does Peter describe what God has done with the crucified Jesus?

options.text: God exalted Him to His right hand as Prince and Savior, to grant repentance and forgiveness to Israel.
options.note: Verse 31 states His exaltation and saving purpose.

options.text: God raised Him up after the leaders had killed Him on a tree.
options.note: True, from verse 30, but the fuller statement adds His exaltation as Prince and Savior granting repentance.

options.text: God appointed Him as a teacher whose example we should follow.
options.note: He is far more than an example; He is the exalted Prince and Savior who grants repentance and forgiveness.

options.text: God has left His future reign uncertain until Israel repents.
options.note: Peter proclaims Him already exalted at God's right hand, not waiting to be enthroned.

quiz.q: What was the substance of Gamaliel's counsel to the Sanhedrin?

options.text: Leave these men alone, for if their work is from God you cannot stop it, and you may be fighting against God.
options.note: Verses 38 to 39 capture his argument from precedent to caution.

options.text: Recall how Theudas and Judas the Galilean rose and came to nothing.
options.note: He cited these examples, but as support for his conclusion to let the apostles go.

options.text: Release the apostles because they had done nothing wrong at all.
options.note: Gamaliel argued from prudence and providence, not from a verdict of innocence; they were still flogged.

options.text: Put the apostles to death at once to end the movement.
options.note: That was the council's first impulse, which Gamaliel argued against.

quiz.q: How did the apostles respond to being flogged and released?

options.text: They rejoiced to be counted worthy of suffering for the Name and kept proclaiming that Jesus is the Christ.
options.note: Verses 41 to 42 show their joy and their unbroken witness.

options.text: They kept teaching daily in the temple and from house to house.
options.note: True, from verse 42, but it omits the striking note that they rejoiced to suffer disgrace for the Name.

options.text: They obeyed the order and stopped speaking for a while.
options.note: They did not stop; they continued teaching every day despite the order.

options.text: They grew discouraged and scattered from Jerusalem.
options.note: They were joyful and steadfast, not discouraged, and remained proclaiming Christ.

questions: Ananias and Sapphira wanted the reputation of total devotion without its reality. Where are you tempted to appear more surrendered to God than you truly are, and what would honesty before Him look like?
Peter said the couple had lied not to men but to God. How does remembering that God sees the heart change the way you approach your giving, your words, and your worship?
The apostles said they must obey God rather than men. Where might your loyalty to Christ come into conflict with a human expectation or authority, and how will you decide?
God exalted Jesus as Prince and Savior to grant repentance. Is there an area where you need to receive His gift of repentance rather than manage your sin on your own?
An angel opened the prison, yet the apostles were still flogged soon after. How do you hold together God's power to rescue and His call to endure suffering for the Name?
The apostles left the council rejoicing to have suffered for Jesus. What would it take for you to count opposition for Christ as an honor rather than merely a burden?

translationNotes.note: The verb rendered kept back means to secretly set apart for oneself or embezzle; the Greek Old Testament uses the same word of Achan in Joshua 7:1, drawing a pointed parallel.
The BSB uses a dash in some editions between brought to the Lord and large numbers; here it is rendered with a comma for readability, with no change of meaning.
Sanhedrin can be rendered the Council; the following phrase, the full assembly of the elders of Israel, is set off with commas in place of the dashes used in some printings.
Hanging Him on a tree draws on Deuteronomy 21:22-23; the language marks Jesus' death as bearing a curse, which Paul unfolds in Galatians 3:13.
Prince translates Archegos, which can also be rendered Leader, Author, or Pioneer; it pictures Christ as the one who goes first and opens the way of life for His people.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 5. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem markers were dropped when building verse text. Em dashes in verses 14, 21, and 26 were converted to commas per the no-dash rule, with no change of meaning. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
